methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $144M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same/adjacent-stage peers in Chips & Semiconductors. Range: $500M - $5.0B.

methods.name: Public-Comp Anchoring
methods.skip_reason: target revenue not on file (private companies rarely disclose)
methods.rationale: Public Semiconductors P/S median is 20.3x across 3 tickers. Cannot apply without target's revenue.

private.name: Substrate
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series A), post-money $1B+

private.name: Rain AI
private.rationale: Same sector (Chips & Semiconductors), same stage (Series A), post-money $500M

data.narrative:
Atomic Machines is a Series A semiconductor frontier-tech company founded by Jeff Holden and Prashant Patil, having raised $144M to date. The valuation exercise draws from a comp universe of 8 private and 3 public semiconductor peers, including Stage A and B companies like Substrate ($1.0B Series A), Rain AI ($500M Series A), Etched ($5.0B Series B), and Positron AI ($1.0B Series B), alongside public anchors (ALAB, NVDA, AMD trading at a 20.3x median P/S).

Two valuation methods triangulated meaningfully. Stage-progression analysis, using total capital raised as a proxy ($144M) and applying a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical for Series A advancement, implies a valuation of $288M. Peer-median analysis across four same-sector, same/adjacent-stage companies yields $1.0B, with the peer range spanning $500M to $5.0B. Public-comp anchoring via P/S multiples could not be applied due to lack of target revenue disclosure. 

The triangulated analysis supports a valuation range of $245M (low) to $1.1B (high), with a median of $644M. Key uncertainties include Atomic Machines' revenue trajectory relative to peers, capital efficiency relative to Stage B valuations, and the company's technical differentiation within the semiconductor subsector. The wide dispersion between stage-progression and peer-median methods reflects the nascent and heterogeneous nature of the frontier-chip opportunity.
